author: @johnnydeep7089
text:
If the graphene made it more thermally conductive then it would have been colder in the end
I propose that this is not observed due to experimental error
Specifically
-the test area was still warm from the previous test
-the temperature measuring apparatus was used improperly (those things specifically say that reflective surfaces will give bad readings)
Though this doesn't mean that the graphene had no effect or that its effect was not positive 
All I'm saying is that your test sucks

author: @ClownWhisper
text:
would this be happening as the result of it simply being a darker solution?
depending on the spectrum emitted from the bulb it could be.
this could be checked by staining a new solution to roughly the same appearance as  the graphene solution.

author: @ArkiverUnifiedEnergy
text: Have you tried salt water? Glass has a very high absorption rate of radiant heat waves even though it is clear. If we had a polished sheet of salt equal to glass most of the radiant heat waves would pass completely through. Salt water may allow radiant energy to travel deeper into solution.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I haven't mate - but this was a comparative - so all the same conditions except one had the graphene and the other didn't - that was the point really
